title: Amenorrhoea — Primary & Secondary
oneLiner: Absence of menstruation — primary (never by age 16) or secondary (≥3 months) — warrants pregnancy test and targeted endocrine workup in all women of reproductive age.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ectopic pregnancy / ruptured ectopic** (in any secondary amenorrhoea + positive pregnancy test) – acute abdominal pain, adnexal tenderness, haemodynamic instability. Single finding: **free fluid on bedside ultrasound** → immediate gynaecology and theatre.
- **Pituitary apoplexy** (in known pituitary tumour, e.g. prolactinoma) – sudden headache, visual field loss, cranial nerve palsy, hypotension. Single finding: **bitemporal hemianopia on confrontation** → urgent MRI pituitary and IV hydrocortisone.
- **Sheehan’s syndrome** (postpartum haemorrhage causing pituitary necrosis) – failure to lactate, hypotension, hypoglycaemia, amenorrhoea. Single finding: **history of massive obstetric haemorrhage** → IV hydrocortisone 100mg stat [check local policy].
sections.Presentation:
- **Primary amenorrhoea:** no menarche by age 16 (or 14 if no secondary sexual characteristics). Often presents with delayed puberty, short stature, or cyclical pain with no bleeding.
- **Secondary amenorrhoea:** absence of menses for ≥3 months in a woman with previously normal cycles. Most common cause is pregnancy.
- **Key symptoms:** galactorrhoea (prolactinoma), hirsutism/acne (PCOS), hot flushes/vaginal dryness (POI), headaches/visual changes (pituitary), weight loss/exercise (hypothalamic).
- **Exam findings:** Tanner staging (primary), signs of hyperandrogenism (PCOS), acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance), visual field testing (bitemporal hemianopia), goitre or thyroid signs, galactorrhoea on breast exam, abdominal/ pelvic mass (pregnancy, large fibroids).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Pregnancy** – most common cause of secondary amenorrhoea. Positive urinary beta-hCG.
- **Hypothalamic amenorrhoea** – low GnRH (stress, weight loss, exercise, eating disorders). Low LH/FSH, low oestrogen. Differentiate by history.
- **PCOS** – anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries. LH:FSH ratio >2:1, elevated free testosterone.
- **Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)** – raised FSH (>25 IU/L) before age 40. Hot flushes, low serum oestradiol.
- **Hyperprolactinaemia** – elevated prolactin, galactorrhoea, pituitary adenoma. Single fasting prolactin (avoid tourniquet/venepuncture stress).
- **Thyroid dysfunction** – hypo- or hyperthyroidism. Check TSH, free T4.
- **Uterine/outflow tract abnormalities** – Asherman syndrome (secondary – history of D&C), imperforate hymen or transverse vaginal septum (primary – cyclical pain, haematocolpos). Ultrasound.
- **Pituitary/hypothalamic tumours** (craniopharyngioma) – MRI if neurological symptoms.
- **Splitter:** In primary amenorrhoea with normal secondary sexual characteristics → outflow obstruction (imperforate hymen, palpable mass) vs androgen insensitivity (absent uterus, 46XY). Bedside: **ultrasound showing uterine absence** → karyotype to confirm.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** urine beta-hCG (mandatory in ALL secondary and any primary with breast development). BMI, blood pressure. Visual field confrontation.
- **Bloods:** FSH, LH, oestradiol, prolactin, TSH, free T4, total testosterone, SHBG (calculate free androgen index), beta-hCG if not done. Karyotype if suspected Turner’s (primary amenorrhoea, short stature).
- **Imaging:** Pelvic ultrasound (transabdominal in virginal, transvaginal if possible) – uterus, ovaries, endometrial thickness, polycystic ovarian appearance. MRI pituitary if prolactin >2x upper limit of normal or visual field defect.
- **Special tests:** Progestogen challenge (medroxyprogesterone 10mg OD for 7-10 days – withdrawal bleed indicates oestrogenised endometrium). Rarely needed if FSH/oestradiol done.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** PCOS by Rotterdam – 2 of 3 (oligo-amenorrhoea, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries). POI – FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions 4 weeks apart + amenorrhoea for 4 months.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – missing it can delay diagnosis of ectopic or ongoing pregnancy.
sections.Management:
#### Immediate (first hour / ED):
- **Ectopic suspected** (positive bHCG, pain, unstable) → immediate gynaecology referral, IV access, crossmatch, bedside USS, beta-hCG.
- **Pituitary apoplexy suspected** → urgent MRI pituitary, IV hydrocortisone 100mg stat, neurosurgical referral.
- **Sheehan’s suspected** → IV hydrocortisone, IV fluids, monitor glucose.

#### Ward (first 24-72h):
- Most patients are stable – focus on diagnosis.
- **Hypothalamic amenorrhoea** – dietitian, psychology, reduce exercise intensity, weight restoration (target BMI >19).
- **PCOS** – start combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) for cycle regulation; metformin if metabolic syndrome (dose: [check local policy]).
- **Hyperprolactinaemia** – dopamine agonist: cabergoline 0.25mg twice weekly, titrate to prolactin (check local protocol). Repeat prolactin in 4 weeks.
- **POI** – hormone replacement therapy with oestrogen + cyclical progesterone (if intact uterus). Oestrogen patch 50mcg/24h + progesterone 10mg days 1-14.
- **Thyroid** – treat underlying condition (levothyroxine for hypo, antithyroid drugs for hyper).

#### Long-term / outpatient:
- **Hypothalamic:** monitor for return of menses; bone density DXA if delayed recovery.
- **PCOS:** COCP long-term; screen for type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia. Fertility referral when desired.
- **POI:** HRT until average menopause age (~51), then reassess; DXA baseline and q2-3 years.
- **Hyperprolactinaemia:** cabergoline long-term; MRI q1-2 years if macroprolactinoma.

#### Default Orders (if admitted):
- Diet: general (unless NBM pending surgery for ectopic or pituitary).
- IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125ml/hr if vomiting/dehydrated [check local policy].
- VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if admitted >24h and no contraindications.
- Observations: NEWS2 baseline, then 4-hourly if stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **COCP** – ethinylestradiol 30mcg/levonorgestrel 150mcg daily for 21 days, 7-day break (e.g. Microgynon 30, Rigevidon). – standard dose.
- **Metformin** – start 500mg OD, increase to 500mg TDS or 1g BD over weeks [check local policy]. CI: eGFR <30.
- **Cabergoline** – 0.25mg twice weekly, increase by 0.25mg monthly until prolactin normal (max 1mg twice weekly). CI: known valvular heart disease (echo before high doses).
- **HRT for POI** – oestrogen patch (Estradot 50mcg twice weekly) + cyclical progesterone (Utovestan 10mg PO days 1-14 of calendar month).
- **IV Hydrocortisone (pituitary apoplexy/Sheehan’s)** – 100mg stat, then 50mg q6h IV, convert to oral when stable [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cabergoline** – starting too high or too rapid titration causes severe nausea, postural hypotension. **Metformin** – can cause lactic acidosis if started in acute illness or eGFR <30. **HRT** – omitting progesterone in POI with intact uterus leads to endometrial hyperplasia.
sections.Complications: - **Acute:** Ruptured ectopic (hypovolaemic shock), pituitary apoplexy (visual loss, adrenal crisis), Sheehan’s (life-threatening hypotension/hypoglycaemia).

rawMarkdown:
## Amenorrhoea — Primary & Secondary
**One-liner:** Absence of menstruation — primary (never by age 16) or secondary (≥3 months) — warrants pregnancy test and targeted endocrine workup in all women of reproductive age.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ectopic pregnancy / ruptured ectopic** (in any secondary amenorrhoea + positive pregnancy test) – acute abdominal pain, adnexal tenderness, haemodynamic instability. Single finding: **free fluid on bedside ultrasound** → immediate gynaecology and theatre.
- **Pituitary apoplexy** (in known pituitary tumour, e.g. prolactinoma) – sudden headache, visual field loss, cranial nerve palsy, hypotension. Single finding: **bitemporal hemianopia on confrontation** → urgent MRI pituitary and IV hydrocortisone.
- **Sheehan’s syndrome** (postpartum haemorrhage causing pituitary necrosis) – failure to lactate, hypotension, hypoglycaemia, amenorrhoea. Single finding: **history of massive obstetric haemorrhage** → IV hydrocortisone 100mg stat [check local policy].

### Presentation
- **Primary amenorrhoea:** no menarche by age 16 (or 14 if no secondary sexual characteristics). Often presents with delayed puberty, short stature, or cyclical pain with no bleeding.
- **Secondary amenorrhoea:** absence of menses for ≥3 months in a woman with previously normal cycles. Most common cause is pregnancy.
- **Key symptoms:** galactorrhoea (prolactinoma), hirsutism/acne (PCOS), hot flushes/vaginal dryness (POI), headaches/visual changes (pituitary), weight loss/exercise (hypothalamic).
- **Exam findings:** Tanner staging (primary), signs of hyperandrogenism (PCOS), acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance), visual field testing (bitemporal hemianopia), goitre or thyroid signs, galactorrhoea on breast exam, abdominal/ pelvic mass (pregnancy, large fibroids).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Pregnancy** – most common cause of secondary amenorrhoea. Positive urinary beta-hCG.
- **Hypothalamic amenorrhoea** – low GnRH (stress, weight loss, exercise, eating disorders). Low LH/FSH, low oestrogen. Differentiate by history.
- **PCOS** – anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries. LH:FSH ratio >2:1, elevated free testosterone.
- **Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)** – raised FSH (>25 IU/L) before age 40. Hot flushes, low serum oestradiol.
- **Hyperprolactinaemia** – elevated prolactin, galactorrhoea, pituitary adenoma. Single fasting prolactin (avoid tourniquet/venepuncture stress).
- **Thyroid dysfunction** – hypo- or hyperthyroidism. Check TSH, free T4.
- **Uterine/outflow tract abnormalities** – Asherman syndrome (secondary – history of D&C), imperforate hymen or transverse vaginal septum (primary – cyclical pain, haematocolpos). Ultrasound.
- **Pituitary/hypothalamic tumours** (craniopharyngioma) – MRI if neurological symptoms.
- **Splitter:** In primary amenorrhoea with normal secondary sexual characteristics → outflow obstruction (imperforate hymen, palpable mass) vs androgen insensitivity (absent uterus, 46XY). Bedside: **ultrasound showing uterine absence** → karyotype to confirm.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** urine beta-hCG (mandatory in ALL secondary and any primary with breast development). BMI, blood pressure. Visual field confrontation.
- **Bloods:** FSH, LH, oestradiol, prolactin, TSH, free T4, total testosterone, SHBG (calculate free androgen index), beta-hCG if not done. Karyotype if suspected Turner’s (primary amenorrhoea, short stature).
- **Imaging:** Pelvic ultrasound (transabdominal in virginal, transvaginal if possible) – uterus, ovaries, endometrial thickness, polycystic ovarian appearance. MRI pituitary if prolactin >2x upper limit of normal or visual field defect.
- **Special tests:** Progestogen challenge (medroxyprogesterone 10mg OD for 7-10 days – withdrawal bleed indicates oestrogenised endometrium). Rarely needed if FSH/oestradiol done.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** PCOS by Rotterdam – 2 of 3 (oligo-amenorrhoea, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries). POI – FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions 4 weeks apart + amenorrhoea for 4 months.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – missing it can delay diagnosis of ectopic or ongoing pregnancy.

### Management

#### Immediate (first hour / ED):
- **Ectopic suspected** (positive bHCG, pain, unstable) → immediate gynaecology referral, IV access, crossmatch, bedside USS, beta-hCG.
- **Pituitary apoplexy suspected** → urgent MRI pituitary, IV hydrocortisone 100mg stat, neurosurgical referral.
- **Sheehan’s suspected** → IV hydrocortisone, IV fluids, monitor glucose.

#### Ward (first 24-72h):
- Most patients are stable – focus on diagnosis.
- **Hypothalamic amenorrhoea** – dietitian, psychology, reduce exercise intensity, weight restoration (target BMI >19).
- **PCOS** – start combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) for cycle regulation; metformin if metabolic syndrome (dose: [check local policy]).
- **Hyperprolactinaemia** – dopamine agonist: cabergoline 0.25mg twice weekly, titrate to prolactin (check local protocol). Repeat prolactin in 4 weeks.
- **POI** – hormone replacement therapy with oestrogen + cyclical progesterone (if intact uterus). Oestrogen patch 50mcg/24h + progesterone 10mg days 1-14.
- **Thyroid** – treat underlying condition (levothyroxine for hypo, antithyroid drugs for hyper).

#### Long-term / outpatient:
- **Hypothalamic:** monitor for return of menses; bone density DXA if delayed recovery.
- **PCOS:** COCP long-term; screen for type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia. Fertility referral when desired.
- **POI:** HRT until average menopause age (~51), then reassess; DXA baseline and q2-3 years.
- **Hyperprolactinaemia:** cabergoline long-term; MRI q1-2 years if macroprolactinoma.

#### Default Orders (if admitted):
- Diet: general (unless NBM pending surgery for ectopic or pituitary).
- IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125ml/hr if vomiting/dehydrated [check local policy].
- VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if admitted >24h and no contraindications.
- Observations: NEWS2 baseline, then 4-hourly if stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **COCP** – ethinylestradiol 30mcg/levonorgestrel 150mcg daily for 21 days, 7-day break (e.g. Microgynon 30, Rigevidon). – standard dose.
- **Metformin** – start 500mg OD, increase to 500mg TDS or 1g BD over weeks [check local policy]. CI: eGFR <30.
- **Cabergoline** – 0.25mg twice weekly, increase by 0.25mg monthly until prolactin normal (max 1mg twice weekly). CI: known valvular heart disease (echo before high doses).
- **HRT for POI** – oestrogen patch (Estradot 50mcg twice weekly) + cyclical progesterone (Utovestan 10mg PO days 1-14 of calendar month).
- **IV Hydrocortisone (pituitary apoplexy/Sheehan’s)** – 100mg stat, then 50mg q6h IV, convert to oral when stable [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cabergoline** – starting too high or too rapid titration causes severe nausea, postural hypotension. **Metformin** – can cause lactic acidosis if started in acute illness or eGFR <30. **HRT** – omitting progesterone in POI with intact uterus leads to endometrial hyperplasia.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Ruptured ectopic (hypovolaemic shock), pituitary apoplexy (visual loss, adrenal crisis), Sheehan’s (life-threatening hypotension/hypoglycaemia).
